is a person who works with [theoretical](/wiki/Theory "Theory") or applied [statistics](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics"). The profession exists in both the [private](/wiki/Private_sector "Private sector") and [public sectors](/wiki/Public_sector "Public sector").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Person who works with theoretical or applied statistics

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Globe icon. | The examples and perspective in this article **may not represent a [worldwide view](/wiki/Wikipedia:WikiProject_Countering_systemic_bias "Wikipedia:WikiProject Countering systemic bias") of the subject**. You may [improve this article](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Statistician&action=edit), discuss the issue on the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Statistician "Talk:Statistician"), or [create a new article](/wiki/Wikipedia:Article_wizard "Wikipedia:Article wizard"), as appropriate. *(April 2022)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

A **statistician** is a person who works with [theoretical](/wiki/Theory "Theory") or applied [statistics](/wiki/Statistics "Statistics"). The profession exists in both the [private](/wiki/Private_sector "Private sector") and [public sectors](/wiki/Public_sector "Public sector").

It is common to combine statistical knowledge with expertise in other subjects, and statisticians may work as employees or as [statistical consultants](/wiki/Statistical_consultant "Statistical consultant").[[1]](#cite_note-ONET-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Statistician&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

According to the United States [Bureau of Labor Statistics](/wiki/Bureau_of_Labor_Statistics "Bureau of Labor Statistics"), as of 2014, 26,970 jobs were classified as *statistician* in the United States. Of these people, approximately 30 percent worked for governments (federal, state, or local).[[3]](#cite_note-bls-3) As of October 2021, the median pay for statisticians in the United States was $92,270.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Additionally, there is a substantial number of people who use statistics and data analysis in their work but have job titles other than *statistician*,[[5]](#cite_note-5) such as [actuaries](/wiki/Actuary "Actuary"), [applied mathematicians](/wiki/Applied_mathematics "Applied mathematics"), [economists](/wiki/Economist "Economist"), [data scientists](/wiki/Data_scientists "Data scientists"), [data analysts](/wiki/Data_analyst "Data analyst") ([predictive analytics](/wiki/Predictive_analytics "Predictive analytics")), [financial analysts](/wiki/Financial_analyst "Financial analyst"), [psychometricians](/wiki/Psychometrician "Psychometrician"), [sociologists](/wiki/Sociologist "Sociologist"), [epidemiologists](/wiki/Epidemiologist "Epidemiologist"), and [quantitative psychologists](/wiki/Quantitative_psychology "Quantitative psychology").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Statisticians are included with the [professions](/wiki/Profession "Profession") in various national and international occupational classifications.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In many countries, including the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), employment in the field requires either a [master's degree](/wiki/Master%27s_degree "Master's degree") in statistics or a related field or a [PhD](/wiki/PhD "PhD").[[1]](#cite_note-ONET-1)

According to one industry professional, "Typical work includes collaborating with [scientists](/wiki/Scientist "Scientist"), providing mathematical modeling, simulations, designing randomized experiments and randomized sampling plans, analyzing experimental or survey results, and forecasting future events (such as sales of a product)."[[9]](#cite_note-9)

According to the BLS, "Overall employment grew 33% from 2016 to 2026, much faster than average for all occupations. Businesses will need these workers to analyze the increasing volume of digital and electronic data."[[10]](#cite_note-10) In October 2021, the CNBC rated it the fastest growing job in science and technology of the next decade, with a projected growth rate of 35.40%.[[11]](#cite_note-11)
